title: Packages and access control

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java packages provide names and an access boundary, while public, protected, package-private, and private control which clients may depend on each type or member.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Implementation classes remain package-private behind one public service interface, reducing the surface callers can couple to. Your goal is to create a coherent package structure and justify each exposed type or member. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Exceptions, resources and I/O; Lambdas, streams and optionals; Classes, fields and methods; Interfaces. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java packages provide names and an access boundary, while public, protected, package-private, and private control which clients may depend on each type or member. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Packages are not a security sandbox, protected includes package and subtype rules, split packages/modules complicate deployment, and public API compatibility persists beyond source organization. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Package-private members are private to one source file, and protected means accessible only by subclasses everywhere.

includes: Organize types and minimize public surface using language access levels.
Create a coherent package structure and justify each exposed type or member.
excludes: Packages are not a security sandbox, protected includes package and subtype rules, split packages/modules complicate deployment, and public API compatibility persists beyond source organization.

outcomes: Create a coherent package structure and justify each exposed type or member.

examples.title: Packages and access control: direct language case
examples.situation: Implementation classes remain package-private behind one public service interface, reducing the surface callers can couple to.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Implementation classes remain package-private behind one public service interface, reducing the surface callers can couple to.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Package-private access is shared across the package; protected combines package access with constrained subtype access, so expose the minimum stable surface.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Package-private access is shared across the package; protected combines package access with constrained subtype access, so expose the minimum stable surface.

examples.title: Packages and access control: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A protected field is replaced with a protected method or narrower collaborator because direct mutable representation would spread subclass coupling.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A protected field is replaced with a protected method or narrower collaborator because direct mutable representation would spread subclass coupling.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Packages are not a security sandbox, protected includes package and subtype rules, split packages/modules complicate deployment, and public API compatibility persists beyond source organization.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Package-private members are private to one source file, and protected means accessible only by subclasses everywhere.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create a coherent package structure and justify each exposed type or member.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Package-private members are private to one source file, and protected means accessible only by subclasses everywhere.
misconceptions.correction: Package-private access is shared across the package; protected combines package access with constrained subtype access, so expose the minimum stable surface.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Package-private members are private to one source file, and protected means accessible only by subclasses everywhere.

analogy.example: a versioned delivery line whose public labels, recipe, inspections, and investigation record make one artifact reproducible can introduce Packages and access control, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: packages, Maven, JUnit, debugging, and project evidence
limitations: access control is not a security boundary and caches, plugins, bytecode, secrets, and platform differences need technical controls

recap.summary: You should now be able to create a coherent package structure and justify each exposed type or member. Keep this boundary in view: Packages are not a security sandbox, protected includes package and subtype rules, split packages/modules complicate deployment, and public API compatibility persists beyond source organization.
